items.title: Subscription plan tier (nominal)
items.scenario: A churn model has `plan_type` with values basic, premium, and enterprise. The team one-hot encodes to three columns; linear logistic regression learns separate log-odds shifts per plan instead of assuming enterprise = 2 × basic.
items.explanation: Plan names are nominal—no natural ordering. One-hot gives each tier its own coefficient without imposing a false numeric scale.

items.title: T-shirt size (ordinal)
items.scenario: Inventory data uses sizes XS, S, M, L, XL. The pipeline maps them to 0–4 in size order for an ordinal regression on fabric usage, because larger sizes consistently consume more material in a roughly monotonic way.
items.explanation: Size has a true order; ordinal encoding is appropriate when the model should treat each step as a similar increment.

items.title: Zip code with target encoding
items.scenario: A house-price model has 8,000 unique zip codes—too many for one-hot. Target encoding replaces each zip with the mean sale price of other homes in that zip, computed with leave-one-out on the training fold and smoothed toward the global mean for zips with fewer than five sales.
items.explanation: Target encoding compresses cardinality but requires leakage-safe estimation; rare zips need smoothing to avoid memorizing noise.

items.title: New category at inference
items.scenario: A payment `provider` column includes "Stripe" and "PayPal" in training. After launch, "ShopPay" appears. The production pipeline maps unknown providers to an `__unknown__` bucket learned at train time instead of crashing or silently zeroing all one-hot columns.
items.explanation: Encoders must define behavior for unseen labels; otherwise serving breaks or rows become indistinguishable from all-zero vectors.
